forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -0,0 +1,39 @@
+---
+assignees:
+- claude-code
+position_column: todo
+position_ordinal: a280
+project: file-edit-tools
+title: edit files — stop restoring original mtime on content edits (defeats cargo/rust-analyzer/diagnostics staleness detection)
+---
+## What
+`edit files` preserves the original file **modification time** across a content-changing edit, so the file's mtime never advances. This defeats every mtime-based staleness check downstream — `cargo`/`make` skip rebuilds, file watchers and rust-analyzer can miss the change, and it is a candidate cause of the inline-diagnostics fold-in coming back empty after an edit.
+
+**Root cause** — `crates/swissarmyhammer-tools/src/mcp/tools/files/edit/mod.rs`, `commit_content`:
+- Lines ~1138-1139 capture `original_modified` / `original_accessed` from the pre-edit file.
+- Line ~1190: after the atomic temp-write + `fs::rename`, it calls `set_file_times(path, original_accessed, original_modified)` and reports `metadata_preserved: true`.
+
+The atomic rename naturally gives the new file a current mtime; this code explicitly overwrites it back to the original. Confirmed in practice: an external agent edited a `.rs` file to a compile error, and `cargo check` returned exit 0 in 0.10s with **no recompile** because the mtime was unchanged — only `touch` forced cargo to see the change. This was very likely intended as part of "preserve permissions/metadata," but preserving the **modification** time on a content edit is wrong: the content DID change, so the mtime must advance.
+
+Note: `write files` (`write/mod.rs` `write_file_atomic`) does NOT restore timestamps — it gets a fresh mtime — so this bug is edit-only. Don't change write.
+
+## Approach
+- In `commit_content`, **stop restoring the modification time** so an edit advances mtime to "now" (the rename's natural mtime). Remove the `original_modified` capture + the `set_file_times` mtime restore. Keep **permission** preservation (the genuinely useful part — the existing perms-on-temp-file step before rename) intact.
+- Access time (`atime`): preserving it is low-value and harmless, but do NOT let preserving atime drag the mtime back. Simplest correct fix is to drop the `set_file_times` call entirely; if atime preservation is desired, set times explicitly as `(original_accessed, FileTime::now())` so mtime still advances.
+- Reconcile the `metadata_preserved` field (`EditResult`, line ~907) and the structured result (`mutation.metadata_preserved`, used in the envelope and asserted at line ~3386): it currently means "timestamps restored." After the fix that's misleading — either drop the field, or redefine it to mean "permissions preserved" and update its doc + the success-response/structured-content assembly and the test at ~3386 accordingly.
+- This is necessary but may not be SUFFICIENT for the silent-diagnostics symptom: rust-analyzer spawn is leader-gated and sessions start lazily (see the `diagnostics` project), so a cold/follower MCP process can still return an empty `DiagnoseOutcome`. Scope THIS task to the mtime bug; cross-reference the diagnostics project for the LSP-warmth angle rather than fixing it here.
+
+## Acceptance Criteria
+- [ ] After an `edit files` content edit, the target file's modification time is **strictly greater** than its pre-edit mtime (mtime advances).
+- [ ] File **permissions** are still preserved across the edit (no regression of the perms-preservation behavior).
+- [ ] Encoding + line-ending preservation and atomicity are unchanged (existing edit tests stay green).
+- [ ] `metadata_preserved` is no longer reported as `true` on the basis of restored timestamps — the field is removed or redefined to "permissions preserved," with its doc and the structured-result assertion updated to match.
+
+## Tests
+- [ ] New regression test in `edit/mod.rs`: create a file, set its mtime to a clearly-old value (e.g. via `filetime::set_file_mtime` to a fixed past `FileTime`), run an edit, assert the new mtime is strictly greater than the old (proves staleness detectors will see the change). Avoid wall-clock-sleep flakiness by seeding an old mtime rather than diffing `now`.
+- [ ] Keep/adjust `test_edit_file_permissions_preservation` (perms still preserved).
+- [ ] Update the structured-result test at ~line 3386 for the new `metadata_preserved` semantics (or its removal).
+- [ ] `cargo nextest run -p swissarmyhammer-tools edit::` green (NEVER plain `cargo test`; doctests via `--doc`); `cargo fmt` + `cargo clippy -p swissarmyhammer-tools -- -D warnings` clean.
+
+## Workflow
+- Use `/tdd` — write the failing mtime-advances test first (it will fail because the current code restores the old mtime), then implement the fix.
\ No newline at end of file

After the fix that's misleading — either drop the field, or redefine it to mean "permissions preserved" and update its doc + the success-response/structured-content assembly and the test at ~3386 accordingly.

Scope THIS task to the mtime bug; cross-reference the diagnostics project for the LSP-warmth angle rather than fixing it here.

-- [ ] `metadata_preserved` is no longer reported as `true` on the basis of restored timestamps — the field is removed or redefined to "permissions preserved," with its doc and the structured-result assertion updated to match.
-

Avoid wall-clock-sleep flakiness by seeding an old mtime rather than diffing `now`.
-- [ ] Keep/adjust `test_edit_file_permissions_preservation` (perms still preserved).
-- [ ] Update the structured-result test at ~line 3386 for the new `metadata_preserved` semantics (or its removal).
-- [ ] `cargo nextest run -p swissarmyhammer-tools edit::` green (NEVER plain `cargo test`; doctests via `--doc`); `cargo fmt` + `cargo clippy -p swissarmyhammer-tools -- -D warnings` clean.
-
-## Workflow
-- Use `/tdd` — write the failing mtime-advances test first (it will fail because the current code restores the old mtime), then implement the fix.
\ No newline at end of file

forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -4,36 +4,38 @@
 position_column: todo
 position_ordinal: a280
 project: file-edit-tools
-title: edit files — stop restoring original mtime on content edits (defeats cargo/rust-analyzer/diagnostics staleness detection)
+title: edit files — drop mtime preservation + the metadata_preserved return field (editing advances mtime)
 ---
 ## What
-`edit files` preserves the original file **modification time** across a content-changing edit, so the file's mtime never advances. This defeats every mtime-based staleness check downstream — `cargo`/`make` skip rebuilds, file watchers and rust-analyzer can miss the change, and it is a candidate cause of the inline-diagnostics fold-in coming back empty after an edit.
+`edit files` preserves the original file **modification time** across a content-changing edit, so the file's mtime never advances — defeating every mtime-based staleness check downstream (`cargo`/`make` skip rebuilds, file watchers and rust-analyzer can miss the change, and it's a candidate cause of the inline-diagnostics fold-in returning empty after an edit). It also reports `metadata_preserved: true` in the result, spending return tokens on something the model does not need.
+
+**Decision (from the user):** editing a file IS editing a file — the mtime must advance, and the result should NOT carry a `metadata_preserved` field. Remove both.

 **Root cause** — `crates/swissarmyhammer-tools/src/mcp/tools/files/edit/mod.rs`, `commit_content`:
-- Lines ~1138-1139 capture `original_modified` / `original_accessed` from the pre-edit file.
-- Line ~1190: after the atomic temp-write + `fs::rename`, it calls `set_file_times(path, original_accessed, original_modified)` and reports `metadata_preserved: true`.
+- ~1138-1139 capture `original_modified` / `original_accessed`.
+- ~1190 `set_file_times(path, original_accessed, original_modified)` restores the old timestamps after the atomic temp-write + `fs::rename`, and reports `metadata_preserved`.

-The atomic rename naturally gives the new file a current mtime; this code explicitly overwrites it back to the original. Confirmed in practice: an external agent edited a `.rs` file to a compile error, and `cargo check` returned exit 0 in 0.10s with **no recompile** because the mtime was unchanged — only `touch` forced cargo to see the change. This was very likely intended as part of "preserve permissions/metadata," but preserving the **modification** time on a content edit is wrong: the content DID change, so the mtime must advance.
+Confirmed in practice: an agent edited a `.rs` file to a compile error and `cargo check` returned exit 0 in 0.10s with NO recompile (mtime unchanged); only `touch` forced cargo to see it.

-Note: `write files` (`write/mod.rs` `write_file_atomic`) does NOT restore timestamps — it gets a fresh mtime — so this bug is edit-only. Don't change write.
+`write files` (`write/mod.rs` `write_file_atomic`) does NOT restore timestamps — it gets a fresh mtime — so this is edit-only. Do not change write.

-## Approach
-- In `commit_content`, **stop restoring the modification time** so an edit advances mtime to "now" (the rename's natural mtime). Remove the `original_modified` capture + the `set_file_times` mtime restore. Keep **permission** preservation (the genuinely useful part — the existing perms-on-temp-file step before rename) intact.
-- Access time (`atime`): preserving it is low-value and harmless, but do NOT let preserving atime drag the mtime back. Simplest correct fix is to drop the `set_file_times` call entirely; if atime preservation is desired, set times explicitly as `(original_accessed, FileTime::now())` so mtime still advances.
-- Reconcile the `metadata_preserved` field (`EditResult`, line ~907) and the structured result (`mutation.metadata_preserved`, used in the envelope and asserted at line ~3386): it currently means "timestamps restored." After the fix that's misleading — either drop the field, or redefine it to mean "permissions preserved" and update its doc + the success-response/structured-content assembly and the test at ~3386 accordingly.
-- This is necessary but may not be SUFFICIENT for the silent-diagnostics symptom: rust-analyzer spawn is leader-gated and sessions start lazily (see the `diagnostics` project), so a cold/follower MCP process can still return an empty `DiagnoseOutcome`. Scope THIS task to the mtime bug; cross-reference the diagnostics project for the LSP-warmth angle rather than fixing it here.
+## Approach (firm decisions)
+1. **Drop modification-time preservation.** Remove the `original_modified`/`original_accessed` capture and the `set_file_times(...)` call in `commit_content` so a content edit gets the rename's natural current mtime. Do NOT add atime preservation back — just let it advance.
+2. **Delete the `metadata_preserved` field entirely** (not redefine — delete, to save return tokens): remove it from `EditResult` (~line 907), from the success/structured result assembly, and from `mutation.metadata_preserved` in the envelope. Update/remove the structured-result test that asserts it (~line 3386).
+3. **KEEP permission re-application as silent behavior.** The temp-write+rename gives the new file default permissions, so the existing step that re-applies the original mode (the `set_permissions` on the temp file before rename) must stay — otherwise editing an executable script (e.g. `0755`) silently downgrades it to `0644`. This is invisible (no return field) and prevents a real regression. Do NOT report it in the result.
+4. This may be necessary-but-not-sufficient for the silent-diagnostics symptom (rust-analyzer is leader-gated/lazy — see the `diagnostics` project). Scope THIS task to the mtime + return-field cleanup; cross-reference diagnostics for the LSP-warmth angle.

 ## Acceptance Criteria
-- [ ] After an `edit files` content edit, the target file's modification time is **strictly greater** than its pre-edit mtime (mtime advances).
-- [ ] File **permissions** are still preserved across the edit (no regression of the perms-preservation behavior).
-- [ ] Encoding + line-ending preservation and atomicity are unchanged (existing edit tests stay green).
-- [ ] `metadata_preserved` is no longer reported as `true` on the basis of restored timestamps — the field is removed or redefined to "permissions preserved," with its doc and the structured-result assertion updated to match.
+- [ ] After an `edit files` content edit, the file's modification time is **strictly greater** than its pre-edit mtime.
+- [ ] The result (text + structured `mutation` envelope) contains **no** `metadata_preserved` field anywhere.
+- [ ] File **permissions are still preserved** across an edit (e.g. a `0755` file stays `0755`) — silent, not reported.
+- [ ] Encoding + line-ending preservation and atomicity unchanged; existing edit tests green.

 ## Tests
-- [ ] New regression test in `edit/mod.rs`: create a file, set its mtime to a clearly-old value (e.g. via `filetime::set_file_mtime` to a fixed past `FileTime`), run an edit, assert the new mtime is strictly greater than the old (proves staleness detectors will see the change). Avoid wall-clock-sleep flakiness by seeding an old mtime rather than diffing `now`.
-- [ ] Keep/adjust `test_edit_file_permissions_preservation` (perms still preserved).
-- [ ] Update the structured-result test at ~line 3386 for the new `metadata_preserved` semantics (or its removal).
+- [ ] New regression test in `edit/mod.rs`: create a file, seed a clearly-old mtime via `filetime::set_file_mtime` (a fixed past `FileTime`, no wall-clock sleep), edit it, assert the new mtime is strictly greater than the seeded old one.
+- [ ] Keep `test_edit_file_permissions_preservation` (perms still preserved after the change).
+- [ ] Remove/replace the `metadata_preserved` assertion at ~line 3386 and any other test asserting that field; grep to confirm no `metadata_preserved` references remain in the crate.
 - [ ] `cargo nextest run -p swissarmyhammer-tools edit::` green (NEVER plain `cargo test`; doctests via `--doc`); `cargo fmt` + `cargo clippy -p swissarmyhammer-tools -- -D warnings` clean.

 ## Workflow
-- Use `/tdd` — write the failing mtime-advances test first (it will fail because the current code restores the old mtime), then implement the fix.
\ No newline at end of file
+- Use `/tdd` — write the failing mtime-advances test first (fails today because the old mtime is restored), then implement.
\ No newline at end of file
